demographic_anchor.name: Donna Whitfield
demographic_anchor.life_stage: single parent, adult child moving out

life_stories.title: Medicaid coverage for her daughter
life_stories.event: When Donna's daughter was born with a mild congenital heart condition, Donna was a single mother earning $26,000 a year. She had no employer health insurance. Medicaid covered her daughter's care — including two specialist visits and a minor corrective procedure at age four. The cardiologist was kind and clear, the appointments were accessible, and the bills never came. Donna has told this story to anyone who complained about 'government healthcare' for twenty years.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government is not uniformly distrusted. Medicaid specifically, and the government safety net generally, is a positive anchor. She is aware of the political rhetoric around these programmes and it makes her angry. Her government trust is moderate, not low.

life_stories.title: Losing her store manager position and getting it back
life_stories.event: After a corporate reorganisation, Donna's store was restructured and her assistant manager title was eliminated. She was offered a shift supervisor role at lower pay. She appealed through HR, documented her performance record, and — after three months of process — was reinstated to a manager position at her original pay. It was exhausting. It worked.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Business institutions can deliver justice when you push. Her trust in her employer is earned and conditional — she knows the process requires active engagement, not passive deference. She respects the company while remaining clear-eyed about how it works.

life_stories.title: Media and the 2020 election
life_stories.event: Donna watched two different family members come to completely opposite conclusions about the 2020 election, each citing news sources that seemed to inhabit different factual universes. Her sister watched Fox exclusively. Her cousin watched MSNBC exclusively. Both were certain. Both were unreachable. Donna stopped watching cable news entirely. She reads local news and checks two national sources for factual reporting, avoiding opinion programming.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Media trust is the lowest of all her institutional categories. She has a practical framework for navigating it — local news over national, factual reporting over opinion — but her baseline trust is very low.

institutional_rank_preference.label: government first (safety net anchor), media last

coping_mechanism.description: Relies on established information sources and trusted personal networks. Changed media consumption to local-only after cable news polarised her family relationships. Routine-based coping to manage information overload.

key_tensions: Government trust elevated by personal safety net experience, contradicting mass population scepticism pattern
Business employer trust conditional and earned through active advocacy for herself, not passively given

narrative: My daughter has a heart condition she was born with. When she was four they did a small procedure to correct it, and she's fine now. The whole thing was covered by Medicaid. I was a single mom making $26,000 a year and the government programme that gets talked about as wasteful and inefficient kept my daughter healthy. So when I hear people say government can't do anything right, I think — well, it did something very right for my family. I'm not naive. I know the VA wait time scandals happened. I know there are programmes that don't work. But I'm not going to say I distrust all government because the political people on TV tell me I should. Media is the thing I actually distrust most. I watched cable news turn my sister and my cousin into people who can't have a conversation anymore. They both think they know what's true and they believe completely different things. I stopped watching all of it. Local news still covers real things. Businesses I have a complicated relationship with. I got pushed out of my manager position once through a reorganisation and had to fight to get it back. The company eventually did the right thing. I appreciate that, but I don't forget that I had to fight for it.
decision_bullets: Government ranks above business — safety net positive experience creates trust anchor that contradicts mass cynicism.
Media is the single lowest trust category — cable news polarisation personally witnessed and experienced.
Business trust earned through demonstrated willingness to correct mistakes when pushed — conditional.
CEO public statements not trusted — perceived as image management rather than substantive claims.
Expert credibility moderate — medical experts from daughter's care are trusted, general expert statements evaluated case by case.
Social media used for news but with conscious scepticism — consumes it while flagging it as unreliable.
Peer network (co-workers, church friends) are the primary trust network for information and decisions.
NGO trust moderate — respects organisations she has directly benefited from or seen deliver.

core_memory.identity_statement: Retail manager and single mother whose government trust is anchored by Medicaid experience; media is her lowest trust category; business conditional.

life_defining_events.event: Medicaid covering daughter's heart condition treatment — complete, accessible, competent care
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Government safety net is a positive institutional anchor; generalised anti-government rhetoric dismissed

life_defining_events.event: Cable news polarising sister and cousin — opposite conclusions from different outlets
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Media trust very low; switched to local news only; avoids opinion programming

trust_network: HR representative who processed her appeal
